content.text:
Lets start with week 4 remaining items. I think it can be grouped into 2 broad categoriees 1. flatfile (#476 & #520) 2. dimention & Score (#124 & #889) 
Plan it out and. then start
I'll start by pulling the details on those four issues.

result.output:
=== #476 ===
[OPEN] profiler: identifier-casing & CSV-delimiter limitations in column introspection/profiling
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement

Surfaced during review of #475 (column-dropdown). These are **pre-existing limitations of the profiler** that the new `list_columns` introspection path inherits/newly surfaces — tracking separately rather than blocking #475.

## 1. Identifier casing (Snowflake / Unity Catalog)
`build_columns_query` issues an unquoted `SELECT * … LIMIT 0`, so `result.keys()` returns the **warehouse-cased** names (Snowflake folds unquoted identifiers to UPPERCASE → `AMOUNT`, not `amount`). The dropdown then offers `AMOUNT`. This is arguably *correct* (those are the real stored names, and the profiler/aggregate path also emits unquoted names that fold the same way), but:
- a **quoted mixed-case** column (`"Amount"`) is stored as `Amount`; the dropdown surfaces `Amount`, but the unquoted profiler/aggregate path folds it to `AMOUNT` and cannot find it. `validate_identifier` also rejects names needing quoting.
- The combobox (#475) lets the author free-type, so this is not a hard block — but quoted/mixed-case identifiers are unsupported end-to-end in the profiler.

**Fix direction:** decide whether v1 supports quoted/mixed-case identifiers; if so, thread a quoting strategy through `validate_identifier` + the Core builders (`_table`, aggregate, top-values, columns) consistently.

## 2. CSV delimiter assumption (flat files)
`pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=0)` (column listing) and `_read_dataframe` (profiling) both use pandas defaults → **comma delimiter**. A `;`/tab-delimited CSV yields one bogus "column" (the whole header line) with no error — a silent wrong answer that defeats the dropdown for that file. Shared by both the profiler and the new lister.

**Fix direction:** sniff the delimiter (`csv.Sniffer` on the header) or accept a delimiter hint on the flat-file connection/target config; apply it consistently in `list_file_columns` and `_read_dataframe`.

Both are low-severity (combobox free-text mitigates #1; #2 matches existing profiler behavior). Post-v1.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Profiler handles case-sensitive/quoted identifiers correctly on all SQL paths
- [ ] CSV delimiter is configurable (or inferred) end-to-end in introspection + profiling
- [ ] Regression tests for both limitations

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #520 ===
[OPEN] Freshness/volume monitors don't support flat-file (ADLS/S3) datasources — SQL-only
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement

## Problem
The freshness & volume monitor kinds (ADR 0012, #426/#437) are evaluated via `run_monitors`, which is implemented **only** on the SQL runners:

- `backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py` — `SnowflakeCheckRunner.run_monitors` (scalar SQL aggregate: `SELECT MAX(<col>)` / `COUNT(*)`)
- `backend/app/datasources/unity_catalog.py` — `UnityCatalogCheckRunner.run_monitors`

`FlatFileCheckRunner` (`backend/app/datasources/flatfile.py`) has **no** `run_monitors`. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` builds the metric as a SQL string (`build_monitor_sql` → `SELECT MAX(column) FROM {target}`), which has no meaning for a flat file read into a DataFrame.

## Impact
- You **cannot** author a freshness (or volume) monitor against an ADLS Gen2 / S3 flat-file connection — the check editor only offers Freshness/Volume for Snowflake/UC.
- Concretely: the demo/test harness (ADR 0021) seeds a deliberate **flat-file freshness** issue — `inventory_snapshot.load_ts` on the latest ADLS Parquet pushed +26h back — and DataQ has **no way to catch it**, because inventory is a flat-file-in-place (Flow C), not a warehouse table. Freshness on the *source* landing zone is arguably the most valuable place to monitor it (it's where "the source stopped producing" first shows up), and it's exactly the gap.
- Discovered while validating live freshness alerting end-to-end (freshness on Snowflake `ORDERS_HEADER.ORDER_TS` works + alerts to Slack; the flat-file equivalent is impossible).

## Direction (not prescriptive)
Give `FlatFileCheckRunner` a `run_monitors` that computes the same metrics without SQL:
- **freshness** — either (a) `MAX(<timestamp column>)` over the resolved batch DataFrame (read the column, take the max), or (b) the **file/blob `last_modified`** of the resolved batch (a "when did this file land" freshness, no column needed). (a) mirrors the SQL semantics; (b) is cheaper and catches "no new file" even when the in-file timestamps are stale/absent. Possibly offer both.
- **volume** — `len(df)` (row count) of the resolved batch — trivial once the batch is read.

The monitor-kind seam (`check.kind`, numeric `metric_value`, ADR 0012) already accommodates this; the change is a runner-level `run_monitors` impl + letting the check editor expose Freshness/Volume for flat-file connections. `monitors.py` would need a non-SQL evaluation path (the age math in `_freshness_age_hours` / `_as_aware_datetime` is already datasource-agnostic and reusable).

## Acceptance
- A freshness monitor can be authored + run against an ADLS/S3 flat-file connection and correctly flags a stale latest batch.
- A volume monitor likewise flags a thinned/short batch.
- The harness's seeded flat-file freshness + volume-drop issues on `inventory_snapshot` are caught.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `freshness` + `volume` monitors run on flat-file connections (S3 + local; ADLS code path covered by tests)
- [ ] Semantics documented (e.g. newest-file mtime for freshness; row/file counts for volume over the matched batch)
- [ ] UI no longer gates monitor kinds to SQL datasources; tests cover the new paths

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #124 ===
[OPEN] Add DQ-dimension classification to checks (Completeness/Uniqueness/Validity/…)
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement,priority/P2

## Context
Checks currently have **two** classification axes, neither of which is a data-quality *dimension*:

| Field | Axis | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `kind` | *how* the monitor works (ADR 0012) | `expectation` / `freshness` / `comparison` |
| `expectation_type` | the specific GX rule | `expect_column_values_to_be_unique` |

A **DQ dimension** (Completeness, Uniqueness, Validity, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness) is a third axis — the *semantic category* of what quality aspect a check measures. It powers dimension-rollup dashboards ("98% complete, 99.5% unique, 95% valid…"), which are a standard, valued DQ-platform feature and would feed the Week-6 health views.

We don't have it today (confirmed: no `dimension` field in the model, roadmap, or ADRs).

## Why deferred (not front-loaded)
Unlike the severity-tier `status` and threshold columns (ADR 0005) or `check.kind`/`metric_value` (ADR 0012) — which had to ride the one-shot Week-3 migration because retrofitting them is an expensive *narrowing*/data-rewrite — a `dimension` column is a **cheap additive nullable column addable anytime** (pure widening, no two-step). So there's no schema-timing pressure; it deserves a deliberate design pass rather than a rushed column in PR-B2.

## Decisions to make first (ADR-worthy)
1. **Taxonomy** — adopt the canonical 6 dimensions? (Completeness, Uniqueness, Validity, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness.) Enum/CHECK vs free-form?
2. **Stored vs auto-derived** — many GX expectations map deterministically to a dimension (`expect_column_values_to_be_unique` → Uniqueness; `…_to_not_be_null` → Completeness; `…_to_be_between`/`…_to_match_regex` → Validity). Do we **store** `dimension` on the check (user-set, override-able) or **derive** it from `expectation_type` via a lookup? Or store with a derived default?
3. **Interaction with `kind`** — freshness/`comparison` monitors map to Timeliness/Consistency respectively; make sure the mapping spans all kinds, not just GX expectations.
4. **Surface** — check editor dropdown (Week 4), dashboard dimension filter/rollup (Week 6).

## Implementation sketch (once decided)
- Additive migration: nullable `checks.dimension` (+ CHECK if enum). No data rewrite, no two-step.
- Add to check CRUD request/response schemas + a `expectation_type → dimension` lookup if auto-deriving.
- Dashboard: `GROUP BY dimension` rollup alongside the ADR-0005 health score.

## When
Post-B2 / alongside the Week-6 dashboard, or whenever dimension reporting becomes a concrete requirement. Decision recorded via PR #123 follow-up (kept out of PR-B2 deliberately).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Every check carries a DQ-dimension classification (auto-derived from expectation/monitor type, user-overridable)
- [ ] Dimension visible in the check editor and results surfaces; export/import round-trips it
- [ ] Backward-compatible migration (existing checks get a derived default)

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #889 ===
[OPEN] feat: asset DQ scorecard — per-dimension coverage + weighted score on the asset page
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement,priority/P2

## Summary
Add a DQ scorecard to the asset detail page (and a score/coverage column on the assets list): per-DQ-dimension view of **which check types are configured** (coverage) and **how many are passing** (score), rolled up over the asset's composing suites' latest runs.

**Depends on #124** (DQ-dimension classification on checks — same milestone). The scorecard is #124's consumer: dimensions land on `Check`, the scorecard aggregates by them.

## Why (feasibility assessed 2026-07-17)
- The check-level view serves the check author; a per-asset score is what a data *consumer* looks at before trusting a table, and what a lead uses to aim DQ effort. It makes the asset page (G-d: #757/#760/#773) the destination view.
- **Coverage gaps are the killer half, not the score**: "this asset has zero Timeliness or Uniqueness checks" is immediately actionable — and most real incidents are freshness/volume (CLAUDE.md §5), i.e. exactly the dimensions most likely to be uncovered.
- ~60% of the substrate exists: `AssetSummaryRead` already rolls up `checks_total`/`checks_passed`/`worst_severity` (asset_view_service._roll_up); `Run.asset_id` is stamped at dispatch so per-asset SQL aggregation over `results` needs no schema change; the ADR-0005 severity-weighted health formula exists in dashboard_service.

## Scope decisions (from the feasibility pass)
- **v1 = coverage view + equal-weight score.** Custom per-dimension weights (workspace default + per-asset override) only if demanded; per-check weights explicitly out of scope — most-configurable-surface, least value.
- **Score semantics:** severity-weighted pass rate per dimension (reuse the ADR-0005 penalty map), computed over composing suites' **latest runs** (same semantics as `_roll_up`); `skip`/`error` excluded from denominators (matches `dashboard_service._SEVERITY_STATUSES`).
- **Authz:** workspace-true score (same number for every viewer who can see the asset), with contributions from suites outside the viewer's grants shown as **redacted counts, not omitted** — the #845/#846 anonymous-neighbour precedent; a per-viewer score would silently disagree between users.
- **Prerequisite refactor:** `asset_view_service._roll_up` and `dashboard_service` are two parallel rollup implementations — unify the shared status/severity math before adding a third consumer.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] #124 landed: `Check.dimension` (derived default + stored override) available to aggregation
- [ ] Rollup unification: one shared severity/status aggregation helper used by dashboard_service, asset_view_service, and the scorecard (no third parallel formula)
- [ ] `GET /assets/{id}` extended with per-dimension scorecard: `{dimension, checks_total, checks_passing, score}` + uncovered-dimensions list
- [ ] Redaction: non-grantee contributions appear as counts only; test pins that browse/detail/scorecard agree (one-rule pattern from #847)
- [ ] Asset page scorecard panel (coverage gaps visually distinct from failing dimensions) + assets-list score column with sort
- [ ] Empty states honest: no checks ≠ score 100 (render "no coverage", not a green tick); stale runs surfaced via `last_run_at`
- [ ] Tests: SQL-level aggregation over seeded multi-suite/multi-dimension fixtures incl. skip/error exclusion + operational-failure axis (#803 split respected)
- [ ] Docs: user-docs asset page section + feature-matrix row

content.text: Four issues, and the milestone headline (#592 `schema_drift`) is already closed — so these are all that's left in W4. Let me recon the code seams before planning.

content.text:
Recon is in. Both groups are well-seamed, but three decisions materially change the build — let me get your call before I start.

**What I found that shapes the plan:**

- **#520 is small** — `run_monitor_specs` in [monitors.py:352](backend/app/datasources/monitors.py#L352) is already the datasource-agnostic seam, and `IcebergCheckRunner.run_monitors` ([iceberg.py:336](backend/app/datasources/iceberg.py#L336)) is a non-SQL template to copy. Blob `last_modified` is already plumbed through `list_files` → `FileRef`. Four touch points: the runner, `check_service.MONITOR_CAPABLE_TYPES:84`, `connections.ts:98`, `run_service.py:203`.
- **#476 is two unrelated bugs.** The CSV delimiter is hardcoded in **four** places (`flatfile.py:117`, `schema_drift.py:203`, `profile_service.py:638`, `:960`) — no `sep=` anywhere in the backend. The casing half is bigger than it reads: `is_sql_identifier` is a deliberate single-point seam, but widening it means `monitors.py:204/240` must gain real quoting, because unlike the profiler's SQLAlchemy Core builders it **f-string-interpolates raw names**.
- **#889 has a prerequisite that's real**: the latest-run-per-suite DISTINCT ON exists twice, and `check_outcome_counts` already computes a four-tier histogram internally ([run_service.py:407](backend/app/services/run_service.py#L407)) and throws it away — which is exactly what a weighted score needs.
- **One flag on #124**: ADR 0036 §4 names #124/#889 and asserts dimension classification is "one story" with turning the frontend expectation catalog into an engine-aware *backend* catalog. I do **not** think we should pull that rewrite in — I'd land `dimension` + a backend derivation map, which is the seed of that catalog, and let ADR 0036 consume it later. Say so if you disagree.

questions.question: #476's casing half asks us to 'decide whether v1 supports quoted/mixed-case identifiers'. Which way?

options.label: Fail loudly + document (Recommended)
options.description: Keep the narrow allowlist, but make the failure honest: introspection flags a column as unsupported instead of offering a name the profiler/monitor can't resolve, and docs state the limitation. Small, no quoting risk on the monitor SQL path.

options.label: Full quoted-identifier support
options.description: Widen is_sql_identifier and thread a dialect-correct quoting strategy through profile_service's Core builders AND monitors.py's f-string SQL + schema_drift. Bigger, and touches every SQL-emitting path at once — with no live Snowflake to verify mixed-case behaviour against.

options.label: Both, column optional (Recommended)
options.description: If the check config names a timestamp column, use MAX(col) over the resolved batch (mirrors SQL semantics); if not, fall back to the resolved file's blob last_modified. Catches 'no new file' AND 'stale rows in a fresh file'.

options.label: File last_modified only
options.description: Cheapest — no read of the file needed. Catches 'the source stopped producing' but misses stale in-file timestamps, and diverges from the SQL freshness semantics.

options.label: Column MAX only
options.description: Exact parity with the Snowflake/UC semantics. But a file that stops arriving entirely is invisible — arguably the more common flat-file incident.

options.label: Derived default, stored override (Recommended)
options.description: Backend expectation_type/kind → dimension map supplies a default at create time; the value is stored on the check and the author can override it in the editor. Matches the issue's AC, survives GX catalog drift, and gives #889 a plain column to GROUP BY.

options.label: Purely derived at read time
options.description: No stored column for the classification — compute from expectation_type on every read. No migration, but no user override, and #889's aggregation must join through a Python map instead of SQL GROUP BY.

options.label: Purely user-set
options.description: Nullable column, author picks it. Simplest code, but existing checks and most new ones land with no dimension, so the scorecard's coverage view is empty until someone backfills by hand.

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "#476's casing half asks us to 'decide whether v1 supports quoted/mixed-case identifiers'. Which way?"="Full quoted-identifier support", "What should flat-file freshness measure (#520)?"="Both, column optional (Recommended)", "How should Check.dimension be populated (#124)?"="Keep it canonical - accuracy, completeness, consistency, validity, timeliness, uniqueness, and integrity. If it can be derived from check type auto-populate it by default. Other wise user can add them in create time. In both cases user can override it at any point of time after check creation.". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text:
Decisions locked. Here's the plan — six PRs, one functionality each per CONTRIBUTING:

**Group 1 — flat-file**
1. **#476a — CSV delimiter.** Collapse the four hardcoded comma reads into one shared reader; delimiter sniffed from the header with an explicit connection/target override winning over the sniff. Applies to `read_dataframe`, `list_file_columns`, profiler `_read_dataframe`, `schema_drift`.
2. **#476b — quoted/mixed-case identifiers, end-to-end.** Widen `is_sql_identifier` (the documented single-point seam) and thread dialect-correct quoting through profile_service's Core builders **and** `monitors.py`/`schema_drift` — the f-string paths are the risky half and get the injection battery.
3. **#520 — flat-file freshness/volume.** `run_monitors` on `FlatFileCheckRunner` via `run_monitor_specs`; freshness = `MAX(col)` when the config names a column, else the resolved file's `last_modified`; volume = row count. Ungate `check_service:84` + `connections.ts:98`.

**Group 2 — dimension & score**
4. **#124 — `Check.dimension`.** ADR for the taxonomy (your canonical seven), additive nullable column + `CheckVersion` snapshot, backend `expectation_type`/`kind` → dimension derivation with user override at create *and* any time after, editor dropdown, export/import round-trip.
5. **#889a — rollup unification** (its own AC): one shared latest-run-per-suite query + one severity/status scorer used by `dashboard_service` and `asset_view_service`; `check_outcome_counts` stops discarding its four-tier histogram.
6. **#889b — the scorecard** on top: per-dimension coverage + weighted score, workspace-true per ADR 0037, asset page panel + list column.

Starting on #476a.

def _read_dataframe(
    connection: Connection,
    *,
    path: str,
    file_format: str,
    columns: list[str],
    secret_store: SecretStore,
) -> Any:
    """Download `path` from the flat-file datasource into a sampled dataframe.

    The live I/O seam (download + parse) — monkeypatched in tests. Applies the two
    "load less data" levers from the pandas scaling guide:

    * **column projection** — only the requested `columns` are parsed (CSV
      `usecols`, Parquet `columns=`), so profiling 3 of 200 columns doesn't read
      all 200. Unknown names are simply not selected; `profile_dataframe` then
      reports genuinely-missing ones as a clean 422.
    * **row sampling** — at most `_SAMPLE_ROWS` rows (CSV pushes the cap into the
      parser; Parquet is sliced after the projected read).

    Not done (deliberate, for an authoring-time sampler): streaming/range reads —
    the whole object is still downloaded before parsing — and out-of-core engines
    (Dask). Both are future work if a profiling-cost problem actually shows up.
    """
    import pandas as pd

    if not connection.secret_ref:
        raise ValueError("connection requires secret_ref for the credential")
    secret = secret_store.get(connection.secret_ref)
    wanted = set(columns)
    raw = io.BytesIO(
        download_bytes(
            conn_type=connection.type, config=connection.config, path=path, secret=secret
        )
    )
    if file_format == "csv":
        return pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=_SAMPLE_ROWS, usecols=lambda name: name in wanted)

    import pyarrow.parquet as pq

    available = set(pq.ParquetFile(raw).schema.names)
    raw.seek(0)
    present = [c for c in columns if c in available]
    # Parquet is already Arrow on disk; dtype_backend="pyarrow" keeps the buffers
    # zero-copy instead of materialising a numpy copy. The stat helpers + the
    # _to_native coercion are Arrow-scalar-safe (min/max → Python int/str,
    # timestamps → Timestamp.isoformat, NA dropped before reductions).
    return pd.read_parquet(raw, columns=present, dtype_backend="pyarrow").head(_SAMPLE_ROWS)

    connection: Connection,
    *,

    file_format: str | None,
    columns: list[str],
    top_n: int,
    secret_store: SecretStore,

def list_file_columns(
    connection: Connection,
    *,
    path: str,
    file_format: str | None,
    secret_store: SecretStore,
) -> list[str]:
    """Column (header) names of a flat file on `connection` (ADLS Gen2 / S3).

    Reads only the header (CSV `nrows=0`) or the Parquet footer schema — no data
    scan. Raises `ProfileTargetInvalidError` (422) for an unknown format and
    `ProfileFailedError` (502) if the file can't be read (exception not echoed).
    """
    import pandas as pd

    # secret_ref presence is guaranteed by the dispatcher (`resolve_profiler`),
    # as in `profile_file`; a direct call without it surfaces as a read failure.
    fmt = infer_file_format(path, file_format)
    try:
        secret = secret_store.get(connection.secret_ref or "")
        raw = io.BytesIO(
            download_bytes(
                conn_type=connection.type, config=connection.config, path=path, secret=secret
            )
        )
        if fmt == "csv":
            return [str(c) for c in pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=0).columns]
        import pyarrow.parquet as pq

        return [str(name) for name in pq.ParquetFile(raw).schema.names]
    except Exception as exc:
        log.warning(
            "column_list_failed", connection_type=connection.type, error_type=type(exc).__name__
        )
        raise ProfileFailedError(
            "columns could not be read from the file", detail={"path": path}
        ) from exc


def list_columns(
    connection: Connection,

    """Column names+types of a flat file: the Parquet footer schema (exact, no

    fmt = infer_file_format(path, file_format)
    secret = secret_store.get(connection.secret_ref or "")
    raw = io.BytesIO(

    if fmt == "csv":
        df = pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=_CSV_TYPE_SAMPLE_ROWS)
        return [{"name": str(col), "type": str(dtype)} for col, dtype in df.dtypes.items()]
    import pyarrow.parquet as pq

    arrow_schema = pq.ParquetFile(raw).schema_arrow

"""AWS S3 connection adapter.

A flat-file datasource (CLAUDE.md §4): DQ checks run against files in a bucket.
Week 2 ships only the `ConnectionAdapter` seam (config validation + connectivity
`test`); the GX pandas/S3 `CheckRunner` that reads the files is a Week-3 concern.

**Auth — v1 supports static access keys only.** The roadmap lists IAM role *and*
access key, but an IAM role has no stored secret and is only meaningfully
testable once DataQ runs on AWS with an instance/task role — so it (and the
broader ``connections.secret_ref`` nullability change) is deferred to Week 7. An
``iam_role`` config is accepted by the schema but rejected with a clear message
in v1; use an access key (``access_key_id`` in config, secret access key in the
SecretStore).

``test`` issues ``head_bucket`` — a green test means the credentials authenticate
and the bucket is reachable. ``boto3`` is imported lazily (per ``core/secrets.py``)
so non-S3 deployments don't pay the import cost; retries are disabled so an
auth/endpoint failure surfaces immediately. Like the other adapters it runs live
and fails-soft pending real credentials.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Any, Literal

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, model_validator

# Fail fast rather than hang the request thread on an unreachable endpoint.
_TEST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 10


class S3Config(BaseModel):
    """Non-secret S3 connection config (the secret access key comes from secrets).

    Maps from ``Connection.config``. ``access_key_id`` is the non-secret half of
    the static credential; the secret access key is resolved from the SecretStore
    at test time.
    """

    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    bucket: str
    region: str
    auth_type: Literal["access_key", "iam_role"] = "access_key"
    access_key_id: str | None = None

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def _check_auth(self) -> S3Config:
        if self.auth_type == "iam_role":
            raise ValueError(
                "iam_role auth is deferred to Week 7 (needs an ambient AWS role to "
                "test against); use auth_type='access_key' with an access key in v1"
            )
        if not self.access_key_id:
            raise ValueError("access_key_id is required when auth_type is 'access_key'")
        return self


class S3ConnectionAdapter:
    """`ConnectionAdapter` for AWS S3 — config validation + a head_bucket probe."""
===== ADLS =====
"""ADLS Gen2 (Azure Data Lake Storage) connection adapter.

A flat-file datasource (CLAUDE.md §4): DQ checks run against files in a
container. Week 2 ships only the `ConnectionAdapter` seam (config validation +
connectivity `test`); the GX pandas/ABFS `CheckRunner` that reads the files is a
Week-3 concern.

**Auth — v1 supports SAS only.** The roadmap lists managed-identity *and* SAS,
but managed identity has no stored secret and is only meaningfully testable once
DataQ runs on Azure with an ambient identity — so it (and the broader
``connections.secret_ref`` nullability change) is deferred to Week 7. A
``managed_identity`` config is accepted by the schema but rejected with a clear
message in v1; use a SAS token (held in the SecretStore).

``test`` builds a ``BlobServiceClient`` from the account URL + SAS and reads the
container's properties — a green test means the endpoint is reachable, the SAS
authenticates, and the container exists. The Azure SDK is imported lazily (per
``core/secrets.py``) so non-ADLS deployments don't pay the import cost; like the
other adapters it runs live and fails-soft pending real credentials.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Any, Literal

from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, field_validator, model_validator

# Fail fast rather than hang the request thread on an unreachable account.
_TEST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 10


class AdlsConfig(BaseModel):
    """Non-secret ADLS Gen2 connection config (the SAS token comes from secrets).

    Maps from ``Connection.config``. ``account_url`` is the storage endpoint
    (e.g. ``https://<account>.blob.core.windows.net``); ``container`` is the
    filesystem. The SAS token is resolved from the SecretStore at test time.
    """

    model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid")

    account_url: str
    container: str
    auth_type: Literal["sas", "managed_identity"] = "sas"

    @field_validator("account_url")
    @classmethod
    def _http_url(cls, value: str) -> str:
        if not value.startswith(("http://", "https://")):
            raise ValueError("account_url must start with http:// or https://")
        return value.rstrip("/")

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def _managed_identity_deferred(self) -> AdlsConfig:
        if self.auth_type == "managed_identity":
            raise ValueError(
                "managed_identity auth is deferred to Week 7 (needs an ambient Azure "
                "identity to test against); use auth_type='sas' with a SAS token in v1"
            )
        return self

content.text: Design call on the delimiter half: I'm going with **per-file sniffing, no config field**. A connection is a whole bucket/container — different files under it legitimately use different delimiters, so a per-connection hint is the wrong granularity, and sniffing needs no migration, no frontend spec change, and no user action. Candidate delimiters are restricted to `,;\t|` with a comma fallback so a failed sniff degrades to today's behaviour.

_FILE_TYPES = {"adls_gen2", "s3"}

# Sort floor for files the store reports without a modified time.
_MIN_DT = datetime.min.replace(tzinfo=UTC)

# Delimiters `sniff_delimiter` will consider, and the fallback when it can't tell
# (which is also the pre-#476 behaviour, so a failed sniff never regresses a file
# that parses today). Deliberately a short allowlist rather than letting
# `csv.Sniffer` pick freely: given a header like `name,title` it will happily
# nominate `e` or a space as the delimiter, which is a *worse* silent wrong
# answer than assuming a comma.
_CSV_DELIMITERS = ",;\t|"
_DEFAULT_DELIMITER = ","

# How much of the object to hand the sniffer. The header plus a few rows is
# plenty and keeps the decode bounded on a large file.
_SNIFF_BYTES = 64 * 1024


def format_from_path(path: str) -> str | None:
    """Infer the file format from the path extension (`None` if unrecognised)."""
    lower = path.lower()
    if lower.endswith(".csv"):
        return "csv"
    if lower.endswith((".parquet", ".pq")):
        return "parquet"
    return None


def sniff_delimiter(sample: bytes) -> str:
    """Guess a CSV's delimiter from its leading bytes, falling back to a comma.

    Pure (no IO) so the decision is testable without a datasource. Sniffing is
    per-*file* on purpose: a flat-file connection is a whole bucket/container and
    the files under it need not agree on a delimiter, so a per-connection hint
    would be the wrong granularity.

    Never raises — an undecidable sample (single column, empty file, binary junk)
    yields `_DEFAULT_DELIMITER`.
    """
    text = sample.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
    # Sniff over whole lines only: a sample cut mid-row can end in a fragment
    # whose field counts don't line up, which is exactly what Sniffer keys off.
    head, newline, _ = text.rpartition("\n")
    if newline:
        text = head
    if not text.strip():
        return _DEFAULT_DELIMITER
    try:
        return csv.Sniffer().sniff(text, delimiters=_CSV_DELIMITERS).delimiter
    except csv.Error:
        return _DEFAULT_DELIMITER


def read_csv_bytes(raw: io.BytesIO, **kwargs: Any) -> Any:
    """`pd.read_csv` over `raw` with the delimiter sniffed from its header (#476).

    The single CSV-parsing seam for every flat-file path — runner, profiler,
    column lister, schema-drift introspection — so they can never disagree about
    what a file's columns are. Extra `kwargs` (``nrows``, ``usecols``, …) pass
    straight through; the buffer is rewound before parsing, so callers may hand
    over a buffer at any position.
    """
    import pandas as pd

    sep = sniff_delimiter(raw.getvalue()[:_SNIFF_BYTES])
    raw.seek(0)
    return pd.read_csv(raw, sep=sep, **kwargs)

@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ("sample", "expected"),
    [
        (b"a,b,c\n1,2,3\n4,5,6\n", ","),
        (b"a;b;c\n1;2;3\n4;5;6\n", ";"),
        (b"a\tb\tc\n1\t2\t3\n4\t5\t6\n", "\t"),
        (b"a|b|c\n1|2|3\n4|5|6\n", "|"),
    ],
)
def test_sniff_delimiter_detects_each_supported_delimiter(sample: bytes, expected: str) -> None:
    assert flatfile.sniff_delimiter(sample) == expected


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "sample",
    [
        b"",  # empty file
        b"\n\n  \n",  # whitespace only
        b"only_one_column\n1\n2\n",  # nothing to infer a delimiter from
        b"\x00\x01\x02\xff\xfe",  # binary junk (undecodable)
        b"a,b\n",  # header with no data rows
    ],
)
def test_sniff_delimiter_falls_back_to_comma_when_undecidable(sample: bytes) -> None:
    """An unsniffable sample must degrade to the pre-#476 behaviour, never raise —
    a wrong-but-unchanged answer beats a 502 on a file that parses today."""
    assert flatfile.sniff_delimiter(sample) == ","


def test_sniff_delimiter_ignores_a_truncated_trailing_row() -> None:
    """The sniff sample is a byte prefix, so the last line is usually cut. A
    fragment with mismatched field counts is what Sniffer keys off, so it must be
    dropped — otherwise the delimiter flips depending on where the cut landed."""
    assert flatfile.sniff_delimiter(b"a;b;c\n1;2;3\n4;5") == ";"


def test_sniff_delimiter_never_picks_a_delimiter_outside_the_allowlist() -> None:
    """Left free, csv.Sniffer nominates letters/spaces on prose-ish headers. The
    allowlist keeps a bad guess bounded to a comma."""
    assert flatfile.sniff_delimiter(b"name title\nalice engineer\nbob analyst\n") == ","


def test_read_csv_bytes_parses_a_semicolon_file_correctly() -> None:
    df = flatfile.read_csv_bytes(io.BytesIO(b"a;b;c\n1;2;3\n4;5;6\n"))
    assert list(df.columns) == ["a", "b", "c"] and len(df) == 2


def test_read_csv_bytes_rewinds_a_consumed_buffer() -> None:
    """Callers may hand over a buffer already read (the sniff itself consumes it),
    so the parse must not silently see zero bytes."""
    raw = io.BytesIO(b"a;b\n1;2\n")
    raw.read()
    df = flatfile.read_csv_bytes(raw)
    assert list(df.columns) == ["a", "b"] and len(df) == 1


def test_read_csv_bytes_passes_through_reader_kwargs() -> None:
    df = flatfile.read_csv_bytes(io.BytesIO(b"a;b;c\n1;2;3\n4;5;6\n"), nrows=1, usecols=["a", "c"])
    assert list(df.columns) == ["a", "c"] and len(df) == 1

60	The **Column values are of type** expectation's `type_` field is the one place the
61	check editor's "obvious" answer is usually wrong. GX validates it against a
62	*different* type vocabulary depending on which engine actually runs the check — not
63	the type your warehouse/catalog shows you:
64	
65	- **Snowflake** builds a real SQL batch (`SqlAlchemyExecutionEngine`) and string-compares
66	  `type_` against the **fully-qualified dialect type**, not the short column type. A
67	  `NUMBER` column reports as `DECIMAL(38, 0)`; `VARCHAR` reports as `VARCHAR(16777216)`.
68	  Plugging in `NUMBER` or `DECIMAL` alone fails every time.
69	- **Unity Catalog, ADLS Gen2 / S3, and Apache Iceberg** all read the target into a
70	  pandas DataFrame first (`PandasExecutionEngine`). GX first tries an **exact dtype
71	  match**; only when the column's dtype is `object` and `type_` isn't
72	  `object`/`object_`/`O` does it fall back to a **row-wise Python value-type compare**.
73	  In practice:
74	  - Numeric columns report numpy dtypes — `int64`, `float64`, `bool`. **Caveat:** an
75	    integer column containing *any* NULL is upcast to `float64` by the read
76	    (`pd.read_sql_table` / `pd.read_csv`), so a nullable `BIGINT` reports `float64`,
77	    not `int64`.
78	  - String columns on **Unity Catalog and CSV** reads are plain pandas `object` dtype
79	    (these reads are *not* Arrow-backed). Both `type_: object` (exact dtype match) and
80	    `type_: str` (row-wise value-type match) pass — pick either.
81	  - **Parquet and Iceberg** reads *are* Arrow-backed and can report Arrow-flavored
82	    dtype names — calibrate from a dry-run rather than assuming the CSV/UC names.
83	
84	| Datasource | Engine | `type_` guidance |
85	|---|---|---|
86	| Snowflake | SQL (dialect-native) | `DECIMAL(38, 0)` for `NUMBER`, `VARCHAR(16777216)` for `VARCHAR` |
87	| Unity Catalog | pandas DataFrame (not Arrow-backed) | `int64` for non-nullable `BIGINT` (**`float64` if the column contains NULLs**); `object` or `str` for `STRING` |
88	| ADLS Gen2 / S3 (CSV) | pandas DataFrame (not Arrow-backed) | `int64`/`float64`/`bool` for numerics (**NULLs upcast integers to `float64`**); `object` or `str` for strings |
89	| ADLS Gen2 / S3 (Parquet) / Iceberg | pandas DataFrame (Arrow-backed) | Arrow-flavored dtype names — confirm via a dry-run's `observed_value` |
90	
91	**Calibration tip:** don't guess — **dry-run first**, but know where the trail runs
92	out. On **Snowflake and the Arrow-backed sources** (Parquet/Iceberg), a failing
93	result's `observed_value` carries the *exact* string GX expected — copy it into
94	`type_` and re-run to confirm green. On **Unity Catalog / CSV**, a wrong value-type
95	guess (e.g. `int64` against a string column) falls to GX's row-wise compare, which
96	fails with **no observed value at all** — the dry-run preview renders Observed as

input.new_string:
### Flat files: formats and CSV delimiters

Flat-file connections (ADLS Gen2 / S3) read `.csv` and `.parquet`/`.pq`. **The CSV
delimiter is detected per file, not per connection** — a connection is a whole
bucket/container and the files under it need not agree, so DataQ sniffs each file's
header. Comma, semicolon, tab, and pipe are recognised; anything it can't decide
(a single-column file, an empty file) is read as comma-separated.

If a file uses some other separator, DataQ will parse the whole header as one
column — the symptom is a **column dropdown offering a single long name** like
`id;email;amount`. Convert the file to one of the four separators, or to Parquet.

content.text: Now the agentic code review before committing, per the working agreements:

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
fix(flatfile): sniff the CSV delimiter per file instead of assuming a comma

Every CSV read in the backend hardcoded the pandas default comma — the runner
(`read_dataframe`), the profiler sample (`_read_dataframe`), the column lister
(`list_file_columns`), and schema-drift introspection. A `;`/tab/pipe-delimited
file therefore parsed as ONE column whose name is the entire header line, with
no error raised: a silent wrong answer that defeats the column dropdown, makes
every check on that file unauthorable, and would report every real column as
simultaneously added and removed the first time drift ran.

Collapses the four sites onto one seam, `flatfile.read_csv_bytes`, which sniffs
the delimiter from the file's header before parsing. Sniffing is per-FILE on
purpose: a flat-file connection is a whole bucket/container and its files need
not share a delimiter, so a per-connection config hint would be the wrong
granularity (and would need a migration + a frontend spec field to express).

`csv.Sniffer` is constrained to an allowlist of `,;\t|` — left free it will
nominate a letter or a space as the delimiter for a prose-ish header, which is a
worse silent wrong answer than assuming a comma. An undecidable sample falls
back to comma, i.e. exactly today's behaviour, so no file that parses now can
regress. The sample is cut to whole lines: a byte-prefix ends mid-row, and a
fragment's mismatched field count is precisely what the sniffer keys off.

Part of #476 (the identifier-casing half follows separately).
EOF
git log --oneline -1

result.output: 6aedad70 fix(flatfile): sniff the CSV delimiter per file instead of assuming a comma

input.prompt:
Review the single commit on branch `fix/issue-476-csv-delimiter-sniffing` in /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ (diff it against origin/main: `git diff origin/main...HEAD`).

It fixes GitHub issue #476's CSV-delimiter half: every backend CSV read hardcoded the pandas default comma; they now route through a new `flatfile.read_csv_bytes` which sniffs the delimiter via `csv.Sniffer` constrained to `,;\t|`, falling back to comma.

Be adversarial. This project's hard-won lesson is that green tests routinely miss defects — especially state carried across calls, and silent-wrong-answer paths. Specifically probe:
- Correctness of `sniff_delimiter`: quoted fields containing delimiters, a header where a semicolon appears inside a quoted comma-separated field (and vice versa), BOM/UTF-8 prefixes, CRLF line endings, files whose first line is a comment, single-line files, a sample boundary that cuts inside a quoted multi-line field.
- `read_csv_bytes`: is `raw.getvalue()` safe for every caller (are all four call sites really io.BytesIO)? Buffer position/rewind semantics. Does `usecols=lambda` still behave? Interaction with `nrows`.
- Whether routing profiler + schema_drift through a `datasources/` helper creates a layering problem, and whether the removed local `import pandas as pd` lines broke anything else in those functions.
- Any behaviour change for existing comma files that could regress silently (e.g. a comma file that now sniffs as pipe).
- Test quality: do the tests actually pin the failure modes, or only happy paths?

Report concrete findings with file:line. Do not fix anything.

content.text: Two test-coverage findings, both legitimate — the reviewer mutation-tested and confirmed the `_SNIFF_BYTES` bound is entirely unexercised. Fixing both in this PR:

@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ("sample", "expected"),
    [
        # A foreign delimiter inside a quoted field, both directions. This is the
        # exact silent-wrong-answer class the change exists to prevent, so it is
        # pinned rather than left to Sniffer's current behaviour.
        (b'name,note\n"a;b;c",1\n"d;e;f",2\n', ","),
        (b'name;note\n"a,b,c";1\n"d,e,f";2\n', ";"),
        (b"\xef\xbb\xbfa;b\n1;2\n", ";"),  # UTF-8 BOM
        (b"a;b\r\n1;2\r\n3;4\r\n", ";"),  # CRLF
    ],
)
def test_sniff_delimiter_survives_quoting_bom_and_crlf(sample: bytes, expected: str) -> None:
    assert flatfile.sniff_delimiter(sample) == expected

    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
) -> None:
    """`_SNIFF_BYTES` bounds the decode so a multi-GB object doesn't get read into
    a str just to pick a delimiter. Shrink the window rather than build a 64KB
    fixture: the point is that slice-then-trim-to-whole-lines still decides
    correctly when the cut lands mid-row."""
    monkeypatch.setattr(flatfile, "_SNIFF_BYTES", 24)
    raw = io.BytesIO(b"alpha;beta\n" + b"1;2\n" * 500)
    df = flatfile.read_csv_bytes(raw)
    assert list(df.columns) == ["alpha", "beta"] and len(df) == 500

content.text: The mutant **survived** — my test doesn't actually pin the bound. That's the right catch: the slice is a *resource* bound, not a correctness property, so a parse-correctness test can never kill it. Testing it as what it is:

input.old_string:
def test_read_csv_bytes_sniffs_correctly_when_the_file_exceeds_the_sample_window(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
) -> None:
    """`_SNIFF_BYTES` bounds the decode so a multi-GB object doesn't get read into
    a str just to pick a delimiter. Shrink the window rather than build a 64KB
    fixture: the point is that slice-then-trim-to-whole-lines still decides
    correctly when the cut lands mid-row."""
    monkeypatch.setattr(flatfile, "_SNIFF_BYTES", 24)
    raw = io.BytesIO(b"alpha;beta\n" + b"1;2\n" * 500)
    df = flatfile.read_csv_bytes(raw)
    assert list(df.columns) == ["alpha", "beta"] and len(df) == 500
input.new_string:
def test_read_csv_bytes_bounds_the_sniff_sample_on_a_large_file(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
) -> None:
    """`_SNIFF_BYTES` bounds the UTF-8 decode, so the runner — which reads whole
    objects by design — doesn't materialise a multi-GB file as a str just to pick
    a delimiter.

    Asserted by observing the sample handed to the sniffer, not by parse
    correctness: the bound is a RESOURCE property, and a correctness assertion
    passes with or without the slice (verified by mutation), so it would pin
    nothing.
    """
    seen: list[bytes] = []

    def _spy(sample: bytes) -> str:
        seen.append(sample)
        return ";"

    monkeypatch.setattr(flatfile, "sniff_delimiter", _spy)
    raw = io.BytesIO(b"alpha;beta\n" + b"1;2\n" * 20_000)
    df = flatfile.read_csv_bytes(raw)

    assert len(raw.getvalue()) > flatfile._SNIFF_BYTES  # the fixture must exceed it
    assert seen and len(seen[0]) == flatfile._SNIFF_BYTES
    assert list(df.columns) == ["alpha", "beta"] and len(df) == 20_000

input.command:
gh pr create --base main --title "fix(flatfile): sniff the CSV delimiter per file instead of assuming a comma" --milestone "v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## What

Every CSV read in the backend hardcoded the pandas default comma. A `;`/tab/pipe-delimited file therefore parsed as **one column whose name is the entire header line**, with no error raised — the silent-wrong-answer half of #476.

The four sites now route through one seam, `flatfile.read_csv_bytes`, which sniffs the delimiter from the file's header:

| Site | Path |
|---|---|
| `flatfile.read_dataframe` | the runner — every check on the file |
| `profile_service._read_dataframe` | profiler sample (projects by column *name*, so a mis-sniff reports every requested column missing) |
| `profile_service.list_file_columns` | the column dropdown (#475) |
| `schema_drift` flat-file introspection | would report every column added **and** removed on first drift run |

## Design notes

**Sniffing is per-file, not per-connection.** A flat-file connection is a whole bucket/container and its files need not agree on a delimiter, so a config hint would be the wrong granularity — and would need a migration plus a frontend spec field to express. Per-file needs neither, and is correct at the level the ambiguity actually lives.

**`csv.Sniffer` is constrained to `,;\t|`.** Left free it nominates a letter or a space as the delimiter for a prose-ish header — a *worse* silent wrong answer than assuming a comma.

**An undecidable sample falls back to comma**, i.e. exactly today's behaviour, so no file that parses now can regress. The reviewer stress-tested 11 comma files engineered to bait a mis-sniff (semicolons and pipes in every line including the header, tab padding, ragged columns) — all still sniff `,`.

**The sample is trimmed to whole lines.** A byte-prefix ends mid-row and a fragment's mismatched field count is precisely what the sniffer keys off.

## Testing

- `sniff_delimiter`: all four delimiters; undecidable samples (empty, whitespace, single-column, binary junk, header-only) fall back to comma without raising; truncated trailing row ignored; allowlist bound.
- Quoted foreign delimiter in **both** directions, UTF-8 BOM, CRLF.
- `read_csv_bytes`: rewinds a consumed buffer, passes `nrows`/`usecols` through, bounds the sniff sample.
- One `;`-file regression test at **each** of the four call sites.
- Full backend suite green: 2429 passed, 97.16% coverage.

Two of the added tests were **mutation-verified** to fail on the mutant, not just pass clean — the `_SNIFF_BYTES` bound in particular is asserted by observing the sample handed to the sniffer, because it is a resource property that a parse-correctness assertion passes with or without.

## Review

`/code-review` agent run pre-push; both findings (unexercised `_SNIFF_BYTES`, unpinned quoting/BOM/CRLF) fixed in-PR. Nothing deferred.

Part of #476 — the identifier-casing half follows in a separate PR, which will carry the `Fixes`.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"

return f"{_EXPECTATION_PREFIX}{kind}"


class MonitorConfigError(ValueError):
    """A monitor check's config is missing/invalid (bad column, range, or kind)."""


def _ident(name: object, *, what: str) -> str:
    """Validate a SQL identifier (so it's safe to interpolate) and return it.

    Monitor config is user-authored, so the column/table/schema must be validated
    before they touch a query string (no bound-param slot for an identifier). The
    allowlist itself is the shared `datasources.sql` one (#428) — one source of
    truth with the profiler's validator."""
    if not isinstance(name, str) or not is_sql_identifier(name):
        raise MonitorConfigError(f"invalid {what} identifier: {name!r}")
    return name


def qualified_table(*, table: str, schema: str | None, catalog: str | None) -> str:
    """A dotted, identifier-validated ``[catalog.][schema.]table`` for a monitor query.

    A ``catalog`` with no ``schema`` is rejected: skipping the None ``schema`` would
    emit a 2-part ``catalog.table``, which Databricks/Unity Catalog resolves as
    ``schema.table`` (wrong object), not the intended 3-part name. So a catalog
    requires a schema — a misqualified-name footgun raised as a clear config error
    rather than a confusing "table not found" at query time."""
    if catalog is not None and schema is None:
        raise MonitorConfigError(
            f"monitor target {table!r} has a catalog but no schema — "
            "a catalog needs a schema (else catalog.table misresolves as schema.table)"
        )
    parts = [
        _ident(part, what=label)
        for part, label in ((catalog, "catalog"), (schema, "schema"), (table, "table"))
        if part is not None
    ]
    return ".".join(parts)


def build_monitor_sql(
    kind: str, *, table: str, schema: str | None, catalog: str | None, config: dict[str, Any]
) -> str:
    """The scalar-aggregate SQL a SQL runner executes for this monitor.

    ``freshness`` → ``SELECT MAX(<column>) ...``; ``volume`` → ``SELECT COUNT(*) ...``.
    Identifiers are validated (no bind slot for them), so a bad column/table raises
    :class:`MonitorConfigError` rather than building an injectable query. Dispatch
    is the #726 registry — a kind with no scalar-SQL form (the stateful kinds)
    refuses here rather than building a wrong query.
    """
    strategy = _strategy(kind)
    if strategy.build_sql is None:
        raise MonitorConfigError(f"monitor kind {kind!r} has no scalar-SQL form")
    target = qualified_table(table=table, schema=schema, catalog=catalog)
    return strategy.build_sql(target, config)


def _freshness_age_hours(max_timestamp: datetime, now: datetime) -> float:
    """Hours between ``MAX(column)`` and now (clamped at 0 — a clock-skew future
    timestamp isn't 'negatively stale')."""

def run_monitors_over_engine(
    engine: Engine,
    *,
    table: str,
    schema: str | None,
    catalog: str | None,
    monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
    """Run monitor checks over ONE connection from ``engine``, one outcome each.

    The execution edge the SQL runners (Snowflake / Unity Catalog) share (#428):
    opens a single connection and sources every monitor's scalar from it via
    `evaluate_monitors` (a bad monitor errors only itself; a connection-level
    failure propagates and fails the whole run — the open happens before the
    per-monitor loop). The engine's lifecycle (build + dispose) belongs to the
    caller — the seam #427 threads a per-run shared engine through.
    """
    from sqlalchemy import text  # lazy: keep sqlalchemy off this module's import cost

    with engine.connect() as conn:
        return evaluate_monitors(
            lambda sql: conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar(),
            table=table,
            schema=schema,
            catalog=catalog,
            monitors=monitors,
        )

secret_store: SecretStore,
) -> list[ColumnSpec]:
    """Column names+types from ``information_schema.columns`` (Snowflake / UC).

    Identifiers are allowlist-validated before the (only) interpolation — the
    optional catalog prefix; the schema/table land as BOUND parameters, matched
    case-insensitively to mirror how the engines resolve unquoted identifiers
    (the profiler's known #476 casing limits apply here identically).
    """
    effective_schema = resolve_effective_schema(connection, schema)
    validate_identifier(table)
    validate_identifier(effective_schema)
    if catalog is not None:
        validate_identifier(catalog)
    prefix = f"{catalog}." if catalog else ""
    query = text(
        f"SELECT table_schema, table_name, column_name, data_type "  # noqa: S608  # nosec B608 — identifiers validated; values bound
        f"FROM {prefix}information_schema.columns "
        "WHERE UPPER(table_schema) = UPPER(:schema_name) "
        "AND UPPER(table_name) = UPPER(:table_name) "
        "ORDER BY ordinal_position"
    )
    with _open_connection(connection, secret_store) as conn:
        rows = conn.execute(query, {"schema_name": effective_schema, "table_name": table}).all()
    if not rows:
        raise SchemaIntrospectionError(
            f"table {table!r} not found in information_schema (schema {effective_schema!r})"
        )
    # Case-insensitive matching mirrors how the engines resolve unquoted
    # identifiers — but it can match SEVERAL quoted case-variant objects (ORDERS
    # and "Orders"), and merging their columns would baseline a union schema no
    # real table has. Prefer the exact spelling; otherwise demand exactly one
    # distinct object, and refuse (classified) when the reference is ambiguous.
    by_object: dict[tuple[str, str], list[ColumnSpec]] = {}
    for row_schema, row_table, name, data_type in rows:
        by_object.setdefault((str(row_schema), str(row_table)), []).append(
            {"name": str(name), "type": str(data_type)}
        )
    exact = by_object.get((effective_schema, table))
    if exact is not None:
        return exact

content.text:
CI on #934 is green across lint/types/SAST/CodeQL/secrets; backend and frontend tests still running.

Before I build the casing half, a finding that changes its shape — I verified the current behaviour empirically instead of trusting the issue text, and **#476's premise #1 is largely wrong**.

The issue claims "the dropdown surfaces `Amount`, but the unquoted profiler/aggregate path folds it to `AMOUNT` and cannot find it." That isn't what happens. The profiler's SQLAlchemy Core builders already emit dialect-correct quoting:

```
'order_ts'  ->  count(order_ts)      # lowercase left bare → folds to ORDER_TS ✓
'Amount'    ->  count("Amount")      # quoted ✓
'AMOUNT'    ->  count("AMOUNT")      # quoted ✓
'OrderId'   ->  count("OrderId")     # quoted ✓
```

So the profiler is **already correct** for mixed-case. And `schema_drift`'s SQL introspection ([schema_drift.py:145](backend/app/services/schema_drift.py#L145)) already handles casing deliberately — bound params, case-insensitive match, exact-spelling preference, and an explicit refusal when a reference is ambiguous across case-variant objects.

The real defect is confined to **one place**: `monitors.py` f-string-interpolates the raw name with no quoting at all — `f"SELECT MAX({column}) FROM {target}"` ([monitors.py:203](backend/app/datasources/monitors.py#L203)). A quoted mixed-case column is unreachable for freshness monitors, and only there.

That changes the fix. My earlier plan was "widen `is_sql_identifier` + hand-roll quoting at each site" — I now think that's **wrong and unsafe**, for a concrete reason: hand-rolled quoting can't be dialect-agnostic. Snowflake quotes with `"`, but Databricks/Unity Catalog uses backticks and treats `"..."` as a string literal by default. `monitors.py` is deliberately dialect-free, so any `"`-based quoting I write there would silently break Unity Catalog monitors.

The correct fix is to build the monitor SQL through **SQLAlchemy Core and compile it with the engine's dialect**, exactly as the profiler already does. That buys dialect-correct quoting for free, and it's precisely behaviour-preserving — Core leaves lowercase bare, so every existing `order_ts` freshness monitor emits byte-identical SQL, while `Amount` starts working. Widening `is_sql_identifier` then becomes safe because every site interpolates a *quoted* result rather than a raw name.

One thing I want your call on, because it's a user-visible behaviour change I shouldn't decide alone:

questions.question: Widening the identifier allowlist admits names that can only ever be referenced quoted (spaces, dots, unicode, leading digits). On Snowflake a bare `order_ts` currently folds to `ORDER_TS`; once a name is quoted it must match the stored spelling exactly. How far should the widening go?

options.label: Case only, keep the char allowlist (Recommended)
options.description: Fix the monitor-SQL quoting so mixed/upper-case columns work everywhere, but still reject spaces/dots/unicode/leading-digits. Zero behaviour change for existing checks, no new ambiguity, and it closes the actual reported defect. Names needing exotic quoting stay refused — documented, not silently broken.

options.label: Full widening — any quotable identifier
options.description: Admit any name that can be safely quoted (spaces, dots, unicode, leading digits), with dialect-correct quoting at every interpolation site. Maximum capability, but a dotted name like `a.b` becomes genuinely ambiguous against qualified-name parsing, and there is no live warehouse in this session to verify the exotic cases against.

How far should the widening go?"="Case only, keep the char allowlist (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text: Epic checkbox correctly stays unticked (#476 is half-done). Light touch on CLAUDE.md §13:

input.old_string: **Next milestone:** **v1.1 Week 4 — `schema_drift` monitor kind (due 2026-07-31).**
input.new_string: **Next milestone:** **v1.1 Week 4 — `schema_drift` monitor kind (due 2026-08-01) — IN PROGRESS, 4/8.** `schema_drift` itself (#592) shipped 2026-07-17; the remaining four items group into **flat-file** (#476, #520) and **dimension & score** (#124, #889). **#476's delimiter half shipped 2026-07-19 ([#934](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/934))** — every backend CSV read hardcoded the pandas default comma, so a `;`/tab/pipe file parsed as ONE column named after the whole header line, silently: it defeated the column dropdown, made every check on that file unauthorable, and would have reported every real column as simultaneously added *and* removed on the first drift run. All four reads (runner, profiler sample, column lister, drift introspection) now share one `flatfile.read_csv_bytes` seam that sniffs per **file** — a connection is a whole bucket/container and its files need not agree on a delimiter, so a per-connection config hint would have been the wrong granularity (and needed a migration + frontend field to express). `csv.Sniffer` is constrained to `,;\t|` because left free it nominates a *letter or a space* on a prose-ish header — a worse silent wrong answer than assuming a comma; an undecidable sample falls back to comma, i.e. exactly today's behaviour, so nothing that parses now can regress. **#476's casing half was rescoped by checking the code instead of trusting the issue:** the ticket claims the profiler folds `Amount` to `AMOUNT` and can't find it, but the SQLAlchemy Core builders **already** emit dialect-correct quoting (`count("Amount")`, lowercase left bare so it folds naturally), and `schema_drift`'s SQL introspection already handles casing deliberately (bound params, exact-spelling preference, ambiguity refusal). The one genuinely broken path is `monitors.py`, which f-string-interpolates the raw name with **no quoting at all** — so a quoted mixed-case column is unreachable for freshness monitors and nowhere else. The fix is to build monitor SQL through Core and let the dialect quote it; **hand-rolled `"` quoting would be actively wrong for Unity Catalog**, which quotes with backticks and reads `"..."` as a string literal. Widening is scoped to **case only** (user decision) — the character allowlist stays, so spaces/dots/unicode remain refused-and-documented rather than silently broken. **Lesson: the issue text was wrong about which layer was broken; one `compile(dialect=...)` check relocated the whole fix.**

content.text:
Good checkpoint. Let me report where things stand.

## Status

**Plan (6 PRs, unchanged):** flat-file group (#476 delimiter → #476 casing → #520), then dimension & score (#124 → rollup unification → #889 scorecard).

**Shipped:**
- **[#934](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/934) — merged, full CI green.** #476's delimiter half. All four backend CSV reads now share one sniffing seam. Two review findings fixed in-PR, nothing deferred.
- **[#935](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/935) — open.** Docs/progress bookkeeping per the PR-template step.

**Two things worth flagging from the work itself:**

1. **The `_SNIFF_BYTES` test I first wrote was fake.** The reviewer flagged the bound as unexercised; I added a test, then mutation-checked it — and **the mutant survived**. A parse-correctness assertion passes with or without the slice, because the bound is a *resource* property, not a correctness one. Rewrote it to observe the sample handed to the sniffer; it now fails on the mutant. This is the exact failure mode recorded from the #904 watchdog (green tests over a real defect), so I'd rather flag that I walked into it than quietly fix it.

2. **#476's issue text is wrong about which layer is broken**, which I only found by compiling the query builders instead of trusting the description. The profiler already quotes correctly. The defect is one f-string in `monitors.py`. That's why I came back to you on the widening question — and it's also why "hand-roll the quoting" would have been a bug rather than a fix, since Unity Catalog quotes with backticks and reads `"..."` as a string literal.

**Next up (#476 casing):** build monitor SQL through SQLAlchemy Core so the dialect quotes it, add a shared `core_table` helper to `datasources/sql.py` (killing the duplicate table-builder in `profile_service`), and drop the now-unneeded Bandit `S608`/`B608` suppressions since nothing is string-interpolated anymore. Existing lowercase monitors will emit byte-identical SQL — I'll pin that with a compile-comparison test rather than asserting it.

One caveat I want on the record before I build it: **there's no live Snowflake or Databricks in this session**, so I can verify the emitted SQL is dialect-correct by compiling, but I can't prove a quoted mixed-case column actually resolves end-to-end. If you want that verified live, the harness window script would need the Snowflake leg — say the word and I'll scope it in; otherwise I'll note the unverified edge in the PR.

Want me to continue straight into the casing PR, or would you rather I jump to #520 (flat-file monitors — self-contained, no live-warehouse question at all) and come back to casing?
continue

348	        raise MonitorConfigError(f"unknown monitor kind: {kind!r}")
349	    return strategy
350	
351	
352	def run_monitor_specs(
353	    scalar_for: Callable[[MonitorSpec], Any],
354	    *,
355	    monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
356	    now: datetime,
357	) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
358	    """Band a list of monitors given a per-spec scalar source, one ``CheckOutcome``
359	    each, in order. ``scalar_for`` returns the monitor's scalar (``MAX(column)`` /
360	    ``COUNT(*)``) — the only datasource-specific bit: a SQL runner builds+runs a
361	    query (`evaluate_monitors`), the Iceberg runner computes it natively
362	    (``scan().count()`` / a column ``MAX``). DB-free and unit-testable.
363	
364	    A monitor that can't be evaluated — bad column/range (config error) or its
365	    scalar source raised (e.g. unknown column) — yields an ``errored`` outcome for
366	    *that* check only; its siblings still run (mirrors `CheckRunner`'s per-check
367	    `error`, #122). The scalar source must **not** swallow a datasource-establishment
368	    failure (open connection / load catalog): callers do that before the loop so it
369	    propagates and fails the whole run."""
370	    outcomes: list[CheckOutcome] = []
371	    for spec in monitors:
372	        try:
373	            outcomes.append(
374	                monitor_outcome(spec.kind, scalar=scalar_for(spec), config=spec.config, now=now)
375	            )
376	        except Exception as exc:  # one bad monitor errors, never its siblings
377	            outcomes.append(
378	                CheckOutcome(
379	                    expectation_type=monitor_expectation_type(spec.kind),
380	                    success=False,
381	                    errored=True,
382	                    error_message=str(exc),
383	                )
384	            )
385	    return outcomes
386	
387	
388	def evaluate_monitors(
389	    fetch_scalar: Callable[[str], Any],
390	    *,
391	    table: str,
392	    schema: str | None,
393	    catalog: str | None,
394	    monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
395	) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
396	    """Run a list of monitors over an already-open connection via `run_monitor_specs`,
397	    with the scalar sourced from a SQL aggregate. ``fetch_scalar`` runs a SQL string
398	    and returns its scalar — the runner closes over its connection, so this stays
399	    DB-free and unit-testable. Connection *establishment* failure is the runner's
400	    concern (it opens the connection before calling this)."""
401	    now = datetime.now(UTC)
402	
403	    def scalar_for(spec: MonitorSpec) -> Any:
404	        sql = build_monitor_sql(
405	            spec.kind, table=table, schema=schema, catalog=catalog, config=spec.config
406	        )
407	        return fetch_scalar(sql)
408	
409	    return run_monitor_specs(scalar_for, monitors=monitors, now=now)

153	
154	def validate_identifier(name: str | None) -> str:
155	    """Validate `name` against the plain-identifier allowlist and return it.
156	
157	    Raises `ProfileIdentifierInvalidError` (422) for anything that isn't a plain
158	    identifier per the shared `datasources.sql` allowlist (#428 — one source of
159	    truth with the monitor engine's validator). The SQLAlchemy Core builders
160	    quote safely on their own; this is defence-in-depth and turns an odd name
161	    into a clean 422 instead of a quoted column that simply doesn't exist.
162	    """
163	    if name is None or not is_sql_identifier(name):
164	        raise ProfileIdentifierInvalidError(
165	            "not a valid table/schema/column identifier", detail={"identifier": name}
166	        )
167	    return name
168	
169	
170	def _table(schema: str, table_name: str, catalog: str | None = None) -> Any:
171	    """A Core table clause, optionally with a 3-level namespace (Unity Catalog).
172	
173	    With a `catalog`, the namespace is `catalog.schema` passed as an unquoted
174	    `quoted_name` so the dialect emits three dotted parts (`catalog.schema.table`)
175	    rather than quoting the dotted string as one identifier. Safe because every
176	    part is allowlist-validated.
177	    """
178	    validate_identifier(schema)
179	    if catalog is not None:
180	        validate_identifier(catalog)
181	        namespace: Any = quoted_name(f"{catalog}.{schema}", quote=False)
182	    else:
183	        namespace = schema
184	    return table(validate_identifier(table_name), schema=namespace)
185	
186	
187	def build_aggregate_query(
188	    schema: str, table_name: str, columns: list[str], catalog: str | None = None
189	) -> Select[Any]:
190	    """One round-trip: row count + null/distinct/min/max per column.
191	
192	    Built with the Core expression language (no string SQL); identifiers are
193	    validated then handed to `column()`/`table()`, which the dialect quotes.
194	    """
195	    projection: list[Any] = [func.count().label("row_count")]
196	    for i, col in enumerate(columns):
197	        c: Any = column(validate_identifier(col))
198	        projection.append((func.count() - func.count(c)).label(f"nulls_{i}"))
199	        projection.append(func.count(distinct(c)).label(f"distinct_{i}"))
200	        projection.append(func.min(c).label(f"min_{i}"))
201	        projection.append(func.max(c).label(f"max_{i}"))
202	    return select(*projection).select_from(_table(schema, table_name, catalog))

def core_table(*, table: str, schema: str | None, catalog: str | None) -> TableClause:
    """A Core table clause for ``[catalog.][schema.]table`` — the dialect quotes it.

    The one construction shared by the profiler's query builders and the monitor
    engine (#476). Going through Core rather than f-string interpolation is what
    makes a **mixed-case identifier** work at all: the compiler quotes anything
    that isn't already lower-case (``"Amount"``) and leaves a lower-case name bare
    so the warehouse folds it as it always has (``order_ts`` → ``ORDER_TS`` on
    Snowflake). That rule is also why this is behaviour-preserving for every
    identifier that works today.

    It must be Core and not hand-rolled quoting because the quote character is
    **dialect-specific** — Snowflake uses ``"``, Databricks/Unity Catalog uses
    backticks and reads ``"..."`` as a string literal — and this module is shared
    by both.

    With a ``catalog`` the namespace is ``catalog.schema`` passed as an *unquoted*
    ``quoted_name``, so the dialect emits three dotted parts rather than quoting
    the dotted string as one identifier. That one spot is raw interpolation, hence
    the allowlist check on every part here rather than trusting callers (they
    validate first for a good error message; this is the injection guarantee).

    A ``catalog`` with no ``schema`` is refused: dropping the ``None`` schema would
    emit a 2-part ``catalog.table``, which Unity Catalog resolves as
    ``schema.table`` — a wrong object rather than an error.
    """
    from sqlalchemy import table as table_clause
    from sqlalchemy.sql import quoted_name

    if catalog is not None and schema is None:
        raise ValueError(f"table {table!r} has a catalog but no schema")
    for part, label in ((table, "table"), (schema, "schema"), (catalog, "catalog")):
        if part is not None and not is_sql_identifier(part):
            raise ValueError(f"invalid {label} identifier: {part!r}")

    namespace: Any = schema
    if catalog is not None:
        namespace = quoted_name(f"{catalog}.{schema}", quote=False)
    return table_clause(table, schema=namespace)

def _table(schema: str, table_name: str, catalog: str | None = None) -> Any:
    """A Core table clause, optionally with a 3-level namespace (Unity Catalog).

    Construction is the shared `datasources.sql.core_table` (#476 — one builder
    with the monitor engine, so the two can't drift on quoting). Validating here
    first is not redundant: it turns an odd name into a clean 422 with the
    profiler's error shape, where `core_table`'s own guard is the last-resort
    injection check and raises a bare `ValueError`.
    """

        validate_identifier(catalog)

def qualified_table(*, table: str, schema: str | None, catalog: str | None) -> TableClause:
    """An identifier-validated Core table clause for a monitor's target.

    A ``catalog`` with no ``schema`` is rejected: skipping the None ``schema`` would
    emit a 2-part ``catalog.table``, which Databricks/Unity Catalog resolves as
    ``schema.table`` (wrong object), not the intended 3-part name. So a catalog
    requires a schema — a misqualified-name footgun raised as a clear config error
    rather than a confusing "table not found" at query time.

    Validation happens here (for the `MonitorConfigError` message); construction is
    the shared `datasources.sql.core_table`, so the dialect does the quoting."""
    if catalog is not None and schema is None:
        raise MonitorConfigError(
            f"monitor target {table!r} has a catalog but no schema — "
            "a catalog needs a schema (else catalog.table misresolves as schema.table)"
        )
    for part, label in ((catalog, "catalog"), (schema, "schema"), (table, "table")):
        if part is not None:
            _ident(part, what=label)
    return core_table(table=table, schema=schema, catalog=catalog)


def build_monitor_statement(
    kind: str, *, table: str, schema: str | None, catalog: str | None, config: dict[str, Any]
) -> Select[Any]:
    """The scalar-aggregate query a SQL runner executes for this monitor.

    ``freshness`` → ``SELECT MAX(<column>) ...``; ``volume`` → ``SELECT COUNT(*) ...``.

    Returns a **SQLAlchemy Core statement, not a SQL string** (#476). Identifiers
    have no bind slot, so the pre-Core version interpolated the validated name
    directly — which silently folded a quoted mixed-case column (``"Amount"`` was
    emitted bare and resolved as ``AMOUNT``, i.e. not found). Core hands the
    quoting decision to the dialect: lower-case names stay bare and fold exactly as
    they always did, anything else is quoted. Hand-rolled quoting could not fix
    this, because the quote character differs per dialect (Snowflake ``"`` vs
    Databricks backticks).

    A bad column/table raises :class:`MonitorConfigError` rather than building a
    wrong query. Dispatch is the #726 registry — a kind with no scalar form (the
    stateful kinds) refuses here.
    """
    strategy = _strategy(kind)
    if strategy.build_statement is None:
        raise MonitorConfigError(f"monitor kind {kind!r} has no scalar-SQL form")
    target = qualified_table(table=table, schema=schema, catalog=catalog)
    return strategy.build_statement(target, config)

content.text: Now the execution seam:

input.new_string:
def evaluate_monitors(
    fetch_scalar: Callable[[Select[Any]], Any],
    *,
    table: str,
    schema: str | None,
    catalog: str | None,
    monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
    """Run a list of monitors over an already-open connection via `run_monitor_specs`,
    with the scalar sourced from a SQL aggregate. ``fetch_scalar`` executes a Core
    statement and returns its scalar — the runner closes over its connection, so this
    stays DB-free and unit-testable. Connection *establishment* failure is the runner's
    concern (it opens the connection before calling this).

    The statement stays uncompiled all the way to the connection so the **connection's
    own dialect** renders it (#476) — that is what makes identifier quoting correct
    per warehouse instead of guessed here."""
    now = datetime.now(UTC)

    def scalar_for(spec: MonitorSpec) -> Any:
        statement = build_monitor_statement(
            spec.kind, table=table, schema=schema, catalog=catalog, config=spec.config
        )
        return fetch_scalar(statement)

    return run_monitor_specs(scalar_for, monitors=monitors, now=now)

def test_evaluate_monitors_runs_each_in_order() -> None:
    # evaluate_monitors stamps its own `now`, so the freshness timestamp must be
    # relative to real now (not the fixed _NOW). A fake fetch_scalar keys off the
    # SQL: MAX(...) → a ~10h-old timestamp, COUNT → a count.
    def fetch(sql: str) -> object:
        return datetime.now(UTC) - timedelta(hours=10) if "MAX" in sql else 1500

    specs = [
        MonitorSpec(kind="freshness", config={"column": "loaded_at"}),
        MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 1000, "max_rows": 2000}),
    ]
    out = evaluate_monitors(fetch, table="ORDERS", schema="RETAIL", catalog=None, monitors=specs)

    assert [o.expectation_type for o in out] == ["monitor:freshness", "monitor:volume"]
    assert out[0].metric_value == pytest.approx(10.0, abs=0.05)  # freshness age-hours
    assert out[1].metric_value == 0.0  # volume in range


def test_evaluate_monitors_isolates_a_bad_config_monitor() -> None:

input.old_string:
Identifiers are allowlist-validated before the (only) interpolation — the
    optional catalog prefix; the schema/table land as BOUND parameters, matched
    case-insensitively to mirror how the engines resolve unquoted identifiers
    (the profiler's known #476 casing limits apply here identically).
    """
input.new_string:
Identifiers are allowlist-validated before the (only) interpolation — the
    optional catalog prefix; the schema/table land as BOUND parameters, matched
    case-insensitively to mirror how the engines resolve unquoted identifiers.

    The case-insensitive match is deliberate and stays after #476: this queries
    ``information_schema`` for *metadata*, where the table name is a VALUE, not an
    identifier — so a user who typed ``orders`` still finds ``ORDERS``. The
    ambiguity that creates (several case-variant objects) is resolved below by
    preferring the exact spelling and refusing a genuinely ambiguous reference,
    rather than by narrowing the match.
    """

input.old_string: ### Flat files: formats and CSV delimiters
input.new_string:
### Identifier casing (Snowflake / Unity Catalog)

Warehouses fold **unquoted** identifiers — Snowflake upper-cases them — so a column
created as `order_ts` is really stored as `ORDER_TS`, while one created as
`"Amount"` is stored mixed-case and is only reachable quoted.

DataQ handles both: type the name **exactly as the column dropdown reports it**.
Lower-case names are sent unquoted and fold as the warehouse always folded them;
anything else is quoted for you, using the right quote character for the engine
(Snowflake `"`, Databricks backticks). This applies to every SQL path — profiling,
the aggregate/top-values queries, and freshness/volume monitors alike.

**Still unsupported:** identifiers that need quoting for reasons other than case —
spaces, dots, leading digits, or non-ASCII characters. These are refused with a
422 rather than silently mis-resolved. If you have such a column, alias it in a
view and point the check at that.
