content.text:
**Group 1 complete.** Here's where things stand before I start group 2.

## Shipped

| PR | What | State |
|---|---|---|
| [#934](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/934) | #476 — CSV delimiter sniffing | merged |
| [#937](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/937) | #476 — monitor identifier quoting | merged |
| [#940](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/940) | #520 — flat-file freshness & volume | merged |
| [#941](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/941) | tracker | open |

**#476 and #520 are both closed.** W4 is 6/8; remaining is group 2 (#124 → #889). One issue filed and not dropped: [#936](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/936) (3-part catalog names still unquoted — unreachable today, pinned by a test so it reads as a limitation rather than a guarantee).

## Three things you should know

**1. The reviews found real bugs in every PR, and the worst one was mine to catch.** Flat-file column freshness worked on **no Parquet file** — Arrow-backed timestamps (`dtype_backend="pyarrow"`) make `is_datetime64_any_dtype`, `is_object_dtype` and `is_string_dtype` *all* False, so it landed in the numeric-refusal branch and told users their timestamp column wasn't a timestamp. The suite was fully green because every fixture in that file hand-builds a numpy frame — the exact #823 shape. The regression test now rides real Parquet bytes, and I verified it fails against the old condition instead of assuming.

Similarly, #937's first cut **reintroduced the bug it was fixing**: delegating quoting to SQLAlchemy inherited the *dialect's* reserved-word set, which isn't the warehouse's — it reserves `copy`, Snowflake doesn't, so a column stored `COPY` would have stopped resolving on a path that worked before.

**2. Two process misses, both mine, neither buried.** I put a SAS-token-shaped string in a test fixture; GitGuardian correctly failed the PR, and since secret scanning covers history I had to rebuild the branch rather than fix it up. And I ran `git checkout <file>` to undo a mutation test on a file with uncommitted work, destroying all four review fixes — I reapplied them from context and now copy to scratchpad before mutating.

**3. Checking beat trusting, twice.** #476's issue text was wrong about which layer was broken — one `compile(dialect=...)` relocated the whole fix, and turned "widen the allowlist and hand-roll quoting" (which would have been a *bug* on Unity Catalog, where `"..."` is a string literal) into a much smaller, behaviour-preserving change.

## Next: group 2

#124 is the bigger design piece — ADR for your canonical seven dimensions, additive nullable column + `CheckVersion` snapshot, backend derivation map, editor dropdown, export/import round-trip. Then #889's prerequisite rollup unification, then the scorecard.

One thing I'd flag now rather than mid-build: ADR 0036 §4 asserts #124's dimension classification is "one story" with turning the frontend expectation catalog into an engine-aware **backend** catalog. I'm planning to land `dimension` + a backend derivation map as the *seed* of that catalog, and let ADR 0036 consume it later — not pull the catalog rewrite into this PR. Say so if you'd rather I take the larger bite.
Hope all the issues are fixed now and going forward, check the actual code and app. if all the pending issues and items are fixed or filed. Let's start with the group two.
Good call — let me verify against the real code and a running app before moving on, rather than trusting the PR bodies.

A **mostly-unparseable** text column is refused for the same reason: with
    ``errors="coerce"``, 99 junk values and one real date yield a confident metric
    derived from that single row, which then bands as critically stale forever.
    Requiring a majority to parse means "you pointed at the wrong column" surfaces
    as an error instead of a plausible number. A minority of junk still parses
    (it behaves like NULLs, matching ``MAX`` in SQL).

    Caveat, documented rather than guessed at: for *ambiguous* text dates
    (``06/07/2026``) the parse follows pandas' day-first inference, so a
    non-ISO-8601 CSV can be read month-first. Use ISO-8601 or Parquet where the
    distinction matters; a heuristic of our own would just be a second guess.
    """
    import pandas as pd

    cleaned = series.dropna()
    if cleaned.empty:
        return None
    if _is_temporal(cleaned):  # numpy- or Arrow-backed; already an instant
        return cleaned.max()
    # Refuse on NUMERIC, rather than accept only object/string. The inverted form
    # excluded Arrow-backed timestamps (Parquet) along with the numerics, so
    # freshness told users their timestamp column was not a timestamp.
    if pd.api.types.is_numeric_dtype(cleaned) or pd.api.types.is_bool_dtype(cleaned):

            f"({len(parsed)} of {len(cleaned)} values parsed) — check the column name"

def test_a_minority_of_junk_still_parses_like_nulls(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    """The inverse guard: a few bad values behave like NULLs (matching `MAX` in
    SQL), so the threshold must not turn ordinary dirty data into an outage."""
    rows = b"".join(b"%d,2020-01-0%d\n" % (i, (i % 9) + 1) for i in range(9))

    out = _monitor_runner().run_monitors(
        table="raw/orders.csv", schema=None, monitors=[_spec("freshness", column="load_ts")]
    )

Found in a post-merge audit of #520 (PR #940).

## Problem

`FlatFileCheckRunner.run_monitors` computes volume as `len(dataframe())` (`backend/app/datasources/flatfile.py`), and `read_dataframe`'s own docstring says it reads "the **whole** file — every row and column". So a nightly volume monitor on a multi-GB Parquet pulls the entire object into worker memory and fully materialises it as a pandas DataFrame, **every run**, just to produce a row count.

This is the same `#854`-class defect (unbounded read on a scheduled path) that #940 fixed for *freshness* by making `file_last_modified` a single metadata call. Fixed on one leg, left open on the other.

## Direction

- **Parquet**: the footer carries `num_rows` — `pq.ParquetFile(raw).metadata.num_rows` avoids the parse and the DataFrame materialisation entirely. (It does not avoid the *download*; `download_bytes` still pulls the object. A true fix needs range reads, which `profile_service` already lists as deferred work.)
- **CSV**: no footer; a bounded row count still needs a scan, but it can be a streaming line count rather than a full parse into pandas.
- Either way the memo must stay coherent: if a freshness monitor in the same run needs the frame, the download shouldn't happen twice.

## Impact

Correctness is fine — the count is right. This is memory and time on the scheduled worker, which is exactly where #852 showed a 1-CPU container gets starved.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A volume-only monitor run on a Parquet target does not materialise the file as a DataFrame
- [ ] A run mixing volume + column-freshness still reads the object at most once
- [ ] Test asserts the cheap path is taken (observe the parse, as the `_SNIFF_BYTES` test does for the sniff bound) rather than asserting only the count
EOF
)"
gh issue create --title "flat-file batch targets paginate the full prefix on every run" --label enhancement --milestone "v1.1 Backlog" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Found in a post-merge audit of #520 (PR #940). Pre-existing — not a regression from that PR.

## Problem

For a flat-file **batch** target, `run_target.materialize_path` calls `flatfile.resolve_batch_file`, which calls `list_files` — draining every page of the S3 paginator / ADLS `list_blobs` under the batch prefix — on **every run**, before the runner is even built.

#940 removed a *second* unbounded listing from this path (arrival-time freshness now uses `head_object`/`get_blob_properties` on the exact key), but the batch-resolution listing remains. On a prefix with a long history of dated files this grows without bound, on a scheduled path — the `#854` class.

## Direction

Batch resolution genuinely needs to *find* the latest matching key, so it can't be a single head call. But it can be bounded:

- S3 `list_objects_v2` supports `StartAfter` and reverse-ordered narrowing by key prefix; for the common `latest` strategy with a date-ordered key, a bounded page from the end is enough.
- Cap the pages read and surface when the cap truncated the search (`log()` it — a silent cap reads as "we looked at everything", the #839 lesson).
- Or cache the resolved batch per (connection, prefix) for the run.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Batch resolution reads a bounded number of pages for the `latest` strategy
- [ ] Any truncation is logged/surfaced, never silent
- [ ] `specific` strategy still resolves correctly (it can target an old key)
EOF
)"

content.text: Now let me actually run the app, as you asked — tests passing isn't the same as the app working:

input.command:
gh pr create --base main --title "fix(flatfile): refuse a mostly-unparseable freshness column; bound the sniff read" --milestone "v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Two findings from a post-merge audit of #520, **both introduced by that PR**.

## 1. Silent wrong answer — partial-parse freshness

`pd.to_datetime(errors="coerce")` drops what it can't parse, and the guard only fired when *every* value failed. So 99 junk values and one real date produced a **confident metric derived from that single row**, which then bands as critically stale forever.

That's the same class as the epoch trap three lines above it — which exists precisely to stop a non-timestamp column from yielding a plausible number. Verified against the real code before fixing:

```
>>> max_timestamp(pd.Series(['n/a']*99 + ['2020-01-01']), column='load_ts')
2020-01-01 00:00:00+00:00      # no warning, no error
```

A majority must now parse. A minority of junk still behaves like NULLs (matching `MAX` in SQL), so ordinary dirty data doesn't become an outage — pinned in both directions.

**Worth recording:** pandas maps `n/a` to NaN at CSV-parse time, so the obvious fixture is dropped as a NULL *before* the guard and proves nothing. My first test passed for the wrong reason. It now uses `pending`, which pandas keeps as a string and which therefore actually reaches the coercion.

## 2. Peak-memory doubling in the delimiter sniff

`raw.getvalue()[:_SNIFF_BYTES]` copies the **entire** buffer before slicing, so picking a delimiter transiently doubled peak RSS on a large CSV the runner already holds whole in memory. `read(_SNIFF_BYTES)` gets the same bound with no copy — the bound is meant to cap the work, not just the decode.

## Deferred, filed

Two further audit findings are performance, not correctness:

- **#942** — the volume monitor downloads *and fully materialises* the object as a DataFrame just to count rows. Same `#854` class (unbounded read on a scheduled path) that #940 fixed for freshness; fixed on one leg, left open on the other. Parquet's footer carries `num_rows`.
- **#943** — flat-file batch targets paginate the whole prefix on every run. Pre-existing, not from #520.

## Verification

Backend **2480 passed, 97.15%**. Black/Ruff/mypy/Bandit clean.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"

api:
    build:
      context: .

      SECRET_STORE: ${SECRET_STORE:-redis}
      # The dev-bypass identity is the workspace admin locally (non-secret dev
      # default; ADR 0027) so /admin + /settings are drivable in dev and E2E.
      # No-colon form: WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS="" in .env opts back OUT (to a

      WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS: ${WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS-dev-bypass@dataq.local}
      # Request throttling off in the dev stack (#796): dev-bypass traffic

      RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED: ${RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED:-false}
    volumes:

    depends_on:

        condition: service_completed_successfully

    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: backend/Dockerfile
    restart: unless-stopped
    # -B runs celery-beat embedded in the worker (the #171 orchestration polling
    # schedule). Fine for the single-worker dev stack; prod runs a separate beat.
    command: celery -A backend.app.worker.celery_app worker -B --loglevel=INFO
    env_file:
      - path: .env.app
        required: false
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: *database-url
      REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379/0
      # Must match the api service so secrets the API writes are visible here (#86).
      SECRET_STORE: ${SECRET_STORE:-redis}
    volumes:

    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy

        condition: service_completed_successfully

  # React/Vite dev server. corepack pins pnpm from package.json's packageManager.
